title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered in community kitchens. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Grace, The Breakfast Coordinator
Grace coordinated a free breakfast club in a church hall near the bus station, opening before the first offices nearby. [47]She moved the cereal table away from the entrance after noticing that people disliked being seen choosing food as soon as they arrived.[/47] The change made the room feel less like a queue and more like a cafe where people could pause. Grace had worked in event planning, and [48]her experience with seating plans helped her mix regular guests with newcomers without making it obvious.[/48] She learned that conversation often began over practical things, such as finding the jam or sharing a newspaper. The hardest mornings were those when donations arrived late and she had to pretend everything was under control while quietly changing the order of service. Grace says dignity depends on small details: real mugs, warm lighting and volunteers who sit down rather than hover beside the tables.
texts.B:
### Hassan, The Leftovers Driver
Hassan collected boxed surplus restaurant food from local restaurants and delivered it to the community kitchen before evening service, often during the busiest traffic. [49]He had to redesign his route after discovering that one bakery's unsold bread was not ready until the staff had finished cleaning.[/49] Waiting outside taught him more about restaurant schedules than any planning spreadsheet. As a former taxi driver, Hassan knew side streets that avoided football traffic and saved the volunteers from cold meals and anxious cooks. [50]His most awkward delivery involved thirty portions of soup in containers that had not been sealed properly.[/50] After that, he kept crates, towels and spare lids in the van beside the delivery forms. He rarely stayed for dinner, but he enjoyed hearing later that a dish had been built around something he rescued. Hassan says good logistics can taste like generosity when it arrives on time and still hot.
texts.C:
### Nina, The Recipe Adapter
Nina usually helped cooks adapt recipes to whatever ingredients had been donated that week, which could change every afternoon. [51]She stopped writing fixed menus after a planned vegetable stew turned into three different meals because no potatoes arrived.[/51] Instead, she created flexible recipe cards quickly with possible substitutions and notes about cooking times. Nina was studying nutrition, so she checked that meals were filling without relying entirely on cheap pasta, although she respected tight budgets. [52]The biggest challenge was reducing salt when donated bottled sauces and stock cubes were already heavily seasoned.[/52] She began rinsing some ingredients and adding fresh lemon or herbs for brighter flavour. Her favourite compliment came from a guest who asked whether the kitchen could please share the recipe with his daughter. Nina says cooking there taught her that improvisation is not careless; it requires knowing which changes still protect taste and health for everyone eating.
texts.D:
### Brian, The Washing-Up Lead
Brian quietly took charge of washing up because he disliked cooking but wanted the kitchen to run smoothly at the end of service. [53]He introduced separate tubs for plates, cutlery, glasses and pans after volunteers kept blocking the only sink.[/53] The simple system sounded dull, yet it shortened closing time by nearly half an hour on busy nights. Brian had once worked in a hospital laundry, and [54]that experience made him strict about gloves, hot water and clean drying cloths nearby.[/54] Some younger volunteers teased him until an inspection unexpectedly praised the kitchen's overall hygiene. [55]His most difficult moment was telling a popular helper to stop drying dishes with a towel she had used on the floor.[/55] Brian says nobody notices washing up unless it fails, but that suits him perfectly. [56]He feels successful when cooks start the next session with every pan exactly where they expect it each time.[/56]

questions.question: changed the layout to make receiving food feel less exposed?
questions.explanation: Grace moved the cereal table away from the entrance so people were not seen choosing food immediately.

questions.question: used event-planning skills to arrange people socially without drawing attention?
questions.explanation: Grace used seating-plan experience to mix regular guests with newcomers discreetly.

questions.question: altered a collection route after learning one supplier's timing?
questions.explanation: Hassan redesigned his route because the bakery's bread was not ready until after cleaning.

questions.question: recalls a delivery problem caused by containers being insecure?
questions.explanation: Hassan had soup containers that were not properly sealed.

questions.question: abandoned fixed menus after a missing ingredient forced major changes?
questions.explanation: Nina stopped fixed menus after no potatoes arrived for a planned stew.

questions.question: found it hard to control seasoning because donated ingredients were already salty?
questions.explanation: Nina's biggest challenge was reducing salt in sauces and stock cubes.

questions.question: created a sorting system to stop volunteers obstructing a shared facility?
questions.explanation: Brian introduced separate tubs after volunteers blocked the sink.

questions.question: applied hygiene habits learned in a previous workplace?
questions.explanation: Brian's hospital laundry experience made him strict about cleaning procedures.

questions.question: had to correct a well-liked person over an unhygienic action?
questions.explanation: Brian told a popular helper to stop using a floor towel on dishes.

questions.question: measures success by equipment being ready in the right place for others?
questions.explanation: Brian feels successful when pans are exactly where cooks expect them.
